beats.narration_text: Merhaba — this is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. You are working with the mcp-integration skill. It connects Claude Code plugins to external services via Model Context Protocol — four transport types, two config methods, automatic OAuth, and a tool-naming convention that must match character for character. The key decisions: which transport type for the service, dot-mcp-dot-json versus inline mcpServers, and which tools to pre-allow in command frontmatter. Let me walk you through the architecture, the operations, and the gaps.

props.command: Add GitHub SSE MCP server and a local custom stdio server to my plugin.
output: Four transport types: stdio local · SSE hosted OAuth · HTTP token REST · WebSocket real-time
Tool name mcp__plugin_<name>_<server>__<tool> must match exactly — run /mcp before writing frontmatter
OAuth automatic for SSE · ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for all paths · .mcp.json over inline mcpServers

beats.act: anatomy — MCP transport types and lifecycle
beats.narration_text: Four transport types. stdio spawns a local child process — Claude Code manages the lifecycle, communicates via stdin and stdout, and terminates it when done. Use stdio for custom MCP servers, local databases, and NPM-packaged MCP servers. SSE connects to hosted servers via Server-Sent Events — the primary advantage is that OAuth flows are handled automatically. The user authenticates in a browser on first use; Claude Code manages tokens thereafter. Use SSE for official hosted servers like Asana, GitHub, and Linear. HTTP connects to REST API backends with token-based authentication via request headers. Use HTTP for stateless interactions and API backends that do not need persistent connections. WebSocket connects to real-time servers with persistent bidirectional communication — use when low latency or push notifications are required. Lifecycle is the same for all four: plugin loads, configuration is parsed, the server starts or connects, tools are discovered and registered with the full prefix name, and tools become available. Lazy loading applies — not all servers connect at startup; the first tool use triggers connection. Config changes require a Claude Code restart; connection is not live-reloaded.

props.sparkLine: stdio local process. SSE OAuth cloud. HTTP token REST. WebSocket real-time. Lazy load. Restart on config change.

beats.narration_text: Four patterns that matter. First: use dot-mcp-dot-json at the plugin root rather than an inline mcpServers field in plugin.json. The separation of concerns pays off when you have multiple servers — MCP configuration stays isolated and easier to maintain. Second: use the CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT variable for every file path reference. Absolute paths break when a plugin is installed on a different machine or in a different directory; the variable is always expanded to the plugin's installation directory. Third: pre-allow specific MCP tools in command frontmatter — not wildcards. The format is mcp double-underscore plugin-name underscore server-name double-underscore tool-name. Run slash-mcp to discover the exact names before writing the allow list. Fourth: document all required environment variables in the plugin README. Users of stdio and HTTP servers must set these before the plugin works; silent failure on first tool call is worse than explicit setup instructions. Key gotchas. The tool name format is verbose and fragile — one character difference between the pre-allow list and the actual tool name makes the tool invisible with no error. Wildcard pre-allow defeats the permission model — reach for it only when the full list is genuinely unmanageable, not because the list is long. stdio servers must be present at their path before first use: if the plugin bundles a compiled server, users need a build or install step. Config changes require a Claude Code restart — the edit-test loop is slower than for hooks or commands.

props.sparkLine: .mcp.json separation. ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for paths. Pre-allow specifics via /mcp. Document env vars.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The skill gets five things right. Four transport types with documented trade-offs: stdio for local tools, SSE for OAuth cloud services, HTTP for token REST, WebSocket for real-time push — each with explicit use-case guidance rather than a generic list. The CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT variable makes configs portable: no hardcoded path survives across installs, and the variable always expands correctly. OAuth flows are automatic for SSE servers — the user authenticates in a browser once; Claude Code handles tokens thereafter with no additional configuration. The dot-mcp-dot-json separation isolates MCP config from plugin.json, making multi-server plugins maintainable with a clear division of concerns. Pre-allow tool lists in command frontmatter scope permissions: the audit surface stays bounded to explicitly named tools. Where it bites. The tool name format mcp double-underscore plugin-name underscore server-name double-underscore tool-name is verbose and fragile — one typo makes the tool invisible with no error message. No live reload: every config change requires a Claude Code restart, making the edit-test cycle slower than hooks or commands. stdio MCP servers must be present at the expected path — if the plugin bundles a compiled binary, users need a build or install step that the skill does not automate. Wildcard pre-allow defeats the entire permission model; the skill warns against it, but the verbose tool names make it tempting. Authentication tokens in environment variables are documented but not validated at load time — the plugin appears healthy until the first tool call fails silently.

props.sparkLine: Four types, OAuth automatic, portable paths correct. Tool name fragile; no live reload; wildcard bypasses permissions.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. mcp-integration: four transport types — stdio local process, SSE hosted OAuth, HTTP token REST, WebSocket real-time. Use dot-mcp-dot-json over inline mcpServers for multi-server separation. Use CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT for all paths — never hardcode. Pre-allow specific tools in command frontmatter: run slash-mcp to discover exact names. Document all required environment variables in README. Gaps: tool name format verbose and fragile — one typo is invisible with no error; no live reload on config changes; stdio servers must pre-exist at their path; wildcard pre-allow defeats permission model; env var tokens not validated at load time.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Give Claude a task: add a GitHub SSE server and a local custom stdio server to a plugin. Watch five things. First: which config method does it choose — dot-mcp-dot-json or inline mcpServers in plugin.json, and does it prefer the separated file for a multi-server plugin? Second: does it use the CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT variable for all path references, or does it hardcode an absolute path? Third: when pre-allowing tools in command frontmatter, does it run slash-mcp to get exact tool names, or does it guess and risk a silent mismatch? Fourth: for the hosted GitHub server, does it configure SSE type and let OAuth handle authentication automatically? Fifth: does it document the required environment variables for the local stdio server in the plugin README?

Red flag: inline mcpServers · hardcoded paths · wildcard pre-allow · no env var documentation
